title: Matrix Variations: Addition, Search & Diagonal
description: Implement matrix addition, search a 2D array for a target value, and compute the sum of a matrix's diagonal.
congratsText: Great work! You now know how to implement matrix addition, grid search, and diagonal sums.

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
What You Will Learn
By the end of this discussion, you will be able to:
  • Implement matrix addition of two same-size matrices
  • Search a 2D array for a target value and report its position
  • Compute the sum of a matrix's diagonal elements
  • Explain why matrix addition requires equal dimensions

sections.title: Two Chessboards

quiz.question: If two chessboards are overlaid and the piece counts on each square are added together, what operation does this describe?
options: Matrix addition, adding corresponding cells from two grids
Grid search, looking for one specific value
Diagonal sum, adding only the diagonal cells
Row-major traversal, printing one grid after another

sections.lesson:
Two Chessboards
Imagine overlaying two chessboards and adding the piece counts, square by square. That's exactly what matrix addition does — combining two grids by adding the values that share the same position.

sections.title: Matrix Addition Logic

quiz.question: What is the rule for computing result[i][j] in matrix addition?
options: Add the elements from a and b that share the same row and column
Add every element of a to every element of b, regardless of position
Add only the elements along the diagonal of each matrix
Add the row totals of a to the column totals of b

sections.lesson:
Matrix Addition Logic
Each cell of the result is the sum of the two matrices' matching cells — same row, same column, nothing more.
result[i][j] = a[i][j] + b[i][j];

sections.title: Why Matrices Must Be the Same Size

quiz.question: Why does matrix addition require both matrices to have the same number of rows and columns?
options: Every cell needs a matching cell in the other matrix to add to
C only allows matrices with an even number of rows
It doesn't actually require equal sizes — any two matrices can be added
Larger matrices are automatically shrunk to match the smaller one

sections.lesson:
Why Matrices Must Be the Same Size
Since result[i][j] = a[i][j] + b[i][j], every position in a must have a matching position in b. If the dimensions don't match, some cell in one matrix would have no corresponding cell to add.

sections.title: Implementing Matrix Addition

quiz.question: What does result look like after adding a = {{1,2},{3,4}} and b = {{5,6},{7,8}}?

sections.lesson:
Implementing Matrix Addition
The same nested-loop shape from grid traversal now fills in a third matrix, cell by cell.
int a[2][2] = {{1,2},{3,4}}; int b[2][2] = {{5,6},{7,8}}; int result[2][2]; for (int i = 0; i < 2; i++) { for (int j = 0; j < 2; j++) { result[i][j] = a[i][j] + b[i][j]; } } // result = {{6,8},{10,12}}

sections.title: Searching a 2D Array

quiz.question: How does grid search adapt the 1D linear search pattern?
options: It nests the same compare-each-element loop inside a second loop for rows
It requires the matrix to be sorted before it can be searched
It only checks the first row of the matrix, never the rest
It compares entire rows to the target instead of individual cells

sections.lesson:
Searching a 2D Array
Grid search nests the familiar linear-search comparison inside a second loop — every cell in every row gets checked against the target, exactly like 1D search but across two dimensions.
for (int r = 0; r < rows; r++) { for (int c = 0; c < cols; c++) { if (grid[r][c] == target) { printf("Found at row %d, col %d", r, c); } } }

sections.title: Reporting 'Not Found' in a Grid Search

quiz.question: How can a grid search reliably tell the caller that a target was NOT found anywhere in the matrix?
options: Use a found flag that starts false and only becomes true on a match, checked after both loops finish
Assume it was found unless the very first cell doesn't match
It's impossible to detect — grid search always reports success
Only check the last row, since misses always happen there

sections.lesson:
Reporting 'Not Found' in a Grid Search
Since a match could happen on any row or column, the cleanest approach is a boolean-style flag: start it false, set it true (and save the position) on a match, then check it once both loops are done.
int found = 0, foundRow = -1, foundCol = -1; for (int r = 0; r < rows; r++) { for (int c = 0; c < cols; c++) { if (grid[r][c] == target) { found = 1; foundRow = r; foundCol = c; } } } if (!found) printf("Not found");

sections.title: Computing the Diagonal Sum

quiz.question: Which cells make up the main diagonal of a square matrix?
options: The cells where the row index equals the column index, like grid[0][0], grid[1][1]
Every cell in the first row of the matrix
Every cell in the first and last column combined
The cells where the row index is exactly one more than the column index

sections.lesson:
Computing the Diagonal Sum
On a square matrix, the main diagonal is every cell where the row and column index match: grid[0][0], grid[1][1], grid[2][2], and so on. A single loop — not nested — is enough, since each row only contributes one diagonal cell.
int diagSum = 0; for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) { diagSum += grid[i][i]; }

sections.title: Complete Example: Diagonal Sum, Plus a Bonus Challenge

quiz.question: In the bonus challenge of summing only values ABOVE the diagonal, which cells should be included?
options: Cells where the column index is greater than the row index
Cells where the row index is greater than the column index
Every cell in the matrix, with no exceptions
Only the cells directly on the diagonal itself

sections.lesson:
Complete Example: Diagonal Sum, Plus a Bonus Challenge
Extending the diagonal idea, values 'above' the diagonal are the ones where the column index is greater than the row index — visually, the upper-right triangle of the grid.
int aboveSum = 0; for (int r = 0; r < size; r++) { for (int c = 0; c < size; c++) { if (c > r) aboveSum += grid[r][c]; } }

sections.title: Recap

quiz.question: What do matrix addition, grid search, and diagonal sum all have in common?
options: They all build on the same [row][col] indexing and nested-loop traversal introduced earlier
They all require the matrix to be converted into a 1D array first
They each need a completely unrelated, brand-new indexing system
None of them can be implemented using loops at all

sections.lesson:
Recap
Matrix addition combines matching cells from two grids. Grid search nests the linear-search comparison across rows and columns. Diagonal sum picks out cells where the row and column index match. All three reuse the same [row][col] indexing and nested-loop foundation from the previous discussion.
result[i][j] = a[i][j] + b[i][j]; // addition if (grid[r][c] == target) { ... } // search diagSum += grid[i][i]; // diagonal
